question: [File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: fallbackRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger all 10 retry iterations in fallbackRetry() by ensuring every file version in the database fails resultConstructor parsing, causing 10 sequential blocking DB queries per single API request and exhausting the database connection pool?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: fallbackRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user cause fallbackRetry() to execute the maximum 10 iterations by submitting requests that always resolve to corrupt file data rows, causing sustained CPU and DB resource exhaustion that degrades service for all other users?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: fallbackRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user send concurrent requests to getExchangeRate() such that each triggers fallbackRetry() looping up to 10 times, multiplying DB query load by 10x per concurrent request and causing database connection exhaustion?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: fallbackRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the unbounded growth of filters.whereQuery inside fallbackRetry() — where each failed attempt appends a new consensus_timestamp filter — to generate progressively larger SQL WHERE clauses across 10 iterations, causing query parse overhead and memory pressure on the DB?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: fallbackRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that fallbackRetry() never short-circuits when row is null (it returns null immediately) but always retries when resultConstructor throws, to force 10 full DB round-trips per request when the exchange rate file data is consistently malformed?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getLatestFileContentsQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a filterQueries.whereQuery that, after replaceAll('and ', ...) transformation in getLatestFileContentsQuery(), produces an extremely long outerWhere string that causes excessive string allocation and memory pressure in the Node.js process?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getLatestFileContentsQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a whereQuery with many repeated 'and ' substrings such that replaceAll('and ', `and ${FileData.tableAlias}.`) in getLatestFileContentsQuery() performs O(n) string replacements, causing CPU spike during query construction?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getLatestFileDataContents()] Can an unprivileged external user pass a filterQueries object with a very large whereQuery array to getLatestFileDataContents(), causing buildWhereSqlStatement() to allocate a large params array and construct an oversized SQL query string that exhausts Node.js heap memory?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getLatestFileDataContents()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger getLatestFileDataContents() with a whereQuery containing array-valued params (current.param as Array), causing buildWhereSqlStatement() to spread a massive array into params via params.push(...current.param), exhausting the call stack or heap?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: latestFileContentsQuery (static)] Can an unprivileged external user cause the string_agg(f.file_data, '' order by f.consensus_timestamp) in latestFileContentsQuery to aggregate an unbounded number of large file_data chunks into a single string, exhausting PostgreSQL server memory and causing a DB-level OOM crash?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: latestFileContentsQuery (static)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a LIMIT clause in latestFileContentsQuery to force the DB to aggregate all file_data rows since the latest create/update timestamp into a single string_agg result, causing unbounded memory allocation in PostgreSQL?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getFileDataQuery (static)] Can an unprivileged external user supply a fileId and timestamp combination that causes the string_agg in getFileDataQuery to aggregate a very large number of file_data rows, producing a multi-gigabyte result that exhausts PostgreSQL working memory and crashes the query?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getFileDataQuery (static)] Can an unprivileged external user pass an extremely large timestamp value as $2 in getFileDataQuery to force the inner subquery to scan the entire file_data table for a given entity_id, causing a full sequential scan that exhausts DB I/O and CPU?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getFileData()] Can an unprivileged external user call getFileData() with a fileId that has thousands of append transactions between two timestamps, causing string_agg to produce a result that exceeds Node.js string size limits and crashes the process when row.data is accessed?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getFileData()] Can an unprivileged external user repeatedly call getFileData() with different fileId and timestamp combinations that all trigger expensive full-table scans in the inner subquery of getFileDataQuery, causing sustained DB CPU exhaustion that denies service to all other users?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: fallbackRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that fallbackRetry() uses a while loop with ++attempts <= 10 (not async-yielding between iterations) to block the Node.js event loop for the duration of 10 sequential await DB calls, starving other pending requests?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: fallbackRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user cause fallbackRetry() to accumulate a whereQuery array of up to 10 entries — each containing a consensus_timestamp < filter — such that the final SQL query has 10 additional AND conditions, causing the DB query planner to generate an inefficient plan and exhaust CPU?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getExchangeRate()] Can an unprivileged external user repeatedly call the exchange rate API endpoint (which invokes getExchangeRate() -> fallbackRetry()) with no rate limiting, causing each request to issue up to 10 DB queries and collectively exhausting the DB connection pool?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getExchangeRate()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger getExchangeRate() when the exchange rate file (EntityId.systemEntity.exchangeRateFile) has no valid parseable version in the DB, causing fallbackRetry() to always exhaust all 10 attempts and return null, while each attempt issues a full CTE query against the file_data table?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getLatestFileContentsQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a whereQuery whose string representation contains the literal text '<filterInnerPlaceHolder>' or '<filterOuterPlaceHolder>', causing the .replace() calls in getLatestFileContentsQuery() to corrupt the SQL query and trigger a DB error that is retried 10 times in fallbackRetry()?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: getLatestFileContentsQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a whereQuery param value that, after buildWhereSqlStatement() serialization, contains the substring 'and ' such that replaceAll('and ', `and f.`) in getLatestFileContentsQuery() incorrectly prefixes column names in the outerWhere, producing a malformed SQL query that causes a DB error and triggers all 10 fallbackRetry() iterations?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: fallbackRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that fallbackRetry() spreads the original whereQuery array on every failed attempt (filters.whereQuery = [...whereQuery, {...}]) to cause quadratic memory growth — each of 10 iterations copies the growing array — exhausting Node.js heap?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: fallbackRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user cause fallbackRetry() to enter a state where row is never null (DB always returns a row) but resultConstructor always throws, forcing all 10 iterations to execute and issue 10 full CTE queries against the file_data table per single HTTP request?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: latestFileContentsQuery (static)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the CTE latest_create which uses max(consensus_timestamp) with a group by entity_id and order by consensus_timestamp desc to force a full index scan on the file_data table for a high-cardinality entity_id, causing sustained DB CPU exhaustion?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/fileDataService.js -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: latestFileContentsQuery (static)] Can an unprivileged external user trigger the latestFileContentsQuery CTE join (join latest_create l on f.consensus_timestamp >= l.consensus_timestamp) for a file entity with a very old latest create timestamp, causing the join to match an enormous number of rows and exhaust DB sort/aggregate memory?
